questions.question: Explain the difference between FIFO ordering and Causal ordering in group communication.
keyPoints: FIFO: Messages from same sender maintain order at receiver
Causal: Based on happens-before relationship, if send(m2) after receive(m1), then m2 delivered after m1
FIFO is weaker than Causal ordering
Causal ordering implies FIFO but not vice versa

questions.question: What are the three properties required for Consensus? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: Agreement: All correct processes decide on the same value
Validity: The decided value must have been proposed by some process
Termination: Every correct process eventually decides a value

questions.question: Describe the Two-Phase Commit protocol and its two phases.
keyPoints: Phase 1 (Prepare): Coordinator asks participants to prepare for commit
Participants vote Yes or No
Phase 2 (Commit/Abort): If all vote Yes, coordinator sends Commit; otherwise sends Abort
If coordinator fails during phase 1, participants may block

questions.question: What is Virtual Synchrony? Explain its significance in group communication.
keyPoints: Messages sent in one communication step are delivered in that same step to all non-faulty members
Group membership view is consistent across all correct processes
Ensures all processes see the same sequence of group events
Important for building reliable distributed applications

questions.question: Explain the Byzantine Agreement problem. How many processes are needed to tolerate f Byzantine failures?
keyPoints: Byzantine Agreement: Processes must agree on a value despite arbitrary (Byzantine) failures
In Byzantine failures, processes can send incorrect/contradictory information
Required: n > 3f processes to tolerate f Byzantine failures
For 1 Byzantine failure, minimum 4 processes are needed

questions.question: What is the difference between Atomic Multicast and Reliable Multicast?
keyPoints: Atomic Multicast: All-or-nothing delivery - either all receive or none receive
Reliable Multicast: Validity (eventual delivery) + Agreement (if one receives, all receive)
Atomic is stronger than reliable - implies total order and atomicity
Reliable ensures no message loss but doesn't guarantee all-or-nothing

questions.question: Explain the three roles in the Paxos algorithm and their responsibilities.
keyPoints: Proposer: Proposes values, initiates consensus process
Acceptor: Receives proposals, promises to accept if majority agrees, accepts values
Learner: Learns the accepted value once consensus is reached
Majority of acceptors must agree for any decision

questions.question: Describe the Paxos consensus algorithm in detail. Explain how it achieves consensus in the presence of failures. What are its safety and liveness guarantees?
keyPoints: Algorithm uses Prepare and Accept phases
Prepare phase: Proposer sends prepare(n) with proposal number n
Acceptors promise not to accept proposals with number less than n
Accept phase: Proposer sends accept(n, value) to majority of acceptors
If accepted by majority, value becomes chosen
Safety: No two proposals can be chosen (no inconsistency)
Liveness: If a value is proposed, eventually some value will be chosen (requires majority of acceptors working)
Works in asynchronous systems with failures

questions.question: Explain the different types of message ordering in group communication: FIFO, Causal, and Total Order. Provide examples to illustrate each.
keyPoints: FIFO Ordering: Messages from same sender delivered in send order. Example: Process P1 sends m1, m2; receiver delivers m1 then m2
Causal Ordering: Based on happens-before. If m2 is sent after m1 is received, m2 delivered after m1. Example: P1 receives m1, then sends m2; all receivers deliver m1 before m2
Total Order: All receivers see messages in same order. Example: If m1 delivered before m2 at P2, then m1 must be before m2 at P3 also
Relationships: Total order implies causal; causal implies FIFO; all are independent properties

questions.question: Discuss the Group Membership Service in distributed systems. What are its functions and how does it maintain consistent views?
keyPoints: Maintains list of processes in a group (active members)
Handles member join requests - new member added to group
Handles member voluntary leave - graceful removal
Handles member failures - detection and removal
Delivers view updates to all members consistently
View contains: unique view ID and list of member process IDs
Virtual synchrony ensures view changes delivered atomically
Provides membership change notification to applications
Critical for building reliable coordinated distributed applications

questions.question: Explain the Byzantine Generals problem and the Byzantine Agreement algorithm. How does the algorithm achieve agreement despite Byzantine failures?
keyPoints: Byzantine Generals: n generals surrounding city, some may be traitors
Loyal generals must agree on same battle plan despite traitors
Algorithm: Each general collects opinions from others (OM(m) messages)
Recursive message exchange to reach agreement
With n > 3f generals, can tolerate f traitors
Each loyal general computes same decision regardless of traitors
Byzantine Agreement requires: Agreement, Validity, Termination
Impossibility result: No solution for n <= 3f
